explicit_text: I was born and raised in Mount Sterling, Kentucky, and my family has been here for generations. Growing up, Court Day was a big event in our town, and I have fond memories of wandering downtown with friends during those days in the early 1980s. Mount Sterling is where my family settled after moving through nearby counties, and it holds a special place in my heart as both my home and the center of our long local history.

explicit_text: My family has been part of Montgomery County for nearly ninety years in the public school system, starting in 1934. I taught social studies here for 28 years before retiring in 2023, continuing a tradition started by my mother and grandmother. Montgomery County is not just where I live but where my ancestors have deep roots, including connections to early frontier events like the Morgan Station attack.

explicit_text: Kentucky is where my family’s story unfolds, with ancestors moving through various counties like Bath, Nicholas, and Mason before settling in Montgomery County. Back in the early 1800s, my Manley ancestors came to Kentucky, and my grandmother taught in rural Kentucky schools before college degrees were mandatory. Kentucky’s history, from frontier times to modern days, is deeply intertwined with my family’s experiences and my own teaching career.

explicit_text: My dad’s family originally came from Bath County, near Sharpsburg, just a few miles from Montgomery County. Bath County is part of the broader region where my ancestors lived and worked, connecting our family story across these neighboring counties.

explicit_text: Morgan Station is a significant place in my family history because in 1794, my sixth or seventh great-grandmother was murdered there during a Shawnee attack. My ancestor George Becraft was kidnapped at Morgan Station and taken to Detroit before being released after the Battle of Fallen Timbers. This event connects my family directly to the early frontier conflicts in Kentucky and the struggles of settlers in that era.

explicit_text: George Becraft is one of my ancestors who was kidnapped by the Shawnee at Morgan Station in 1794 and taken to Detroit. He was sold to a Scottish merchant who taught him to read and write, and he was released after the Battle of Fallen Timbers. George eventually made his way back to his father, Abraham Becraft, in Kentucky, making his story a remarkable part of my family history.

explicit_text: Doris Nolan is my aunt and the family genealogist who sparked my interest in tracing our ancestry. She began researching our family history back in the 1950s, long before the internet made it easier. Her dedication laid the groundwork for my own genealogical work, especially during the pandemic when I dove deeper into our family’s past.

explicit_text: Court Day in Mount Sterling has been a long-standing festival since 1794, and it was a highlight of my youth in the early 1980s. It was a time when school was out, and my friends and I could roam downtown freely, hunting for baseball cards and comic books among the vendors. The event brought the community together, with crowds sometimes over 100,000 people, creating a sense of freedom and excitement for us kids.

explicit_text: I remember during a school trip to Washington, D.C., around 1982, one of my friends brought a switchblade comb that looked like a knife to the White House tour. The security guards reacted strongly and confiscated the comb, which was quite a memorable moment for us. The White House visit was a highlight of that trip, contrasting with the small-town freedom we enjoyed back home in Mount Sterling.
